loadError.title: Could not load dashboard data
loadError.bodyWithMessage: {message}. The site usually recovers on the next daily refresh.
loadError.bodyDefault: The metrics file is unreachable. The site usually recovers on the next daily refresh.

overview.sectionLede: Key metrics for the selected ASmap build
overview.noPublishedMaps: No published maps found in metrics.json.
overview.noVariantData: Build {name} has no published variant data.

buildSelector.ariaLabel: ASmap build for Overview metrics. Newest builds listed first.

fallbackBadge.label: Filled fallback
fallbackBadge.tooltip: This build did not publish an unfilled variant. Numbers are read from the filled (embedded) file instead.

entries.label: Entries
info: Each entry maps an IP prefix to the autonomous system that announces it.
Read from the unfilled (source data) variant when it is published, and from the filled variant otherwise.

The vs previous delta only shows when both sides use the same encoding. Filled folds neighbouring prefixes that share an AS, cutting the count by roughly 12 percent, so a mixed comparison would look like the map grew or shrank. In practice this only blanks the delta on the rare filled only build whose comparable predecessor is unfilled.
The file size on disk rides along inside the entries chart tooltip.

uniqueAses.label: Unique ASes
info: Number of distinct autonomous systems referenced anywhere in the map.
Filled and unfilled reference the same ASes. Filled adds prefixes, never new ASes, so this number does not depend on the variant.

The vs previous delta uses the same predecessor as the drift card, so all three cards stay lined up on which build they compare against. The drift card spells the predecessor date out on its own delta line.
The bar below shows the share of mapping entries that point at IPv4 versus IPv6 prefixes.

drift.label: Drift vs previous
drift.filledOnlyHint: Source data drift needs an unfilled variant.
info: Share of routable IPv4 address space whose ASN assignment changed between this build and the most recent build it can be diffed against. A 1 percent drift means about 1 IPv4 address in 100 now lands on a different autonomous system.

The headline is IPv4 because Bitcoin Core peer reachability leans on IPv4 most directly. The IPv6 drift rides along as a second line because the two address families mean different things for peer diversity and cannot be added together. IPv6 is larger than IPv4 by 2 to the power of 96, so a combined number would round IPv4 down to noise.
Worked out from unfilled against unfilled diffs, so real source data drift stays apart from shifts caused by the fill heuristic.

history.sectionLede: Per release drift and entry count trends across every published build
history.emptyDrift: Need at least two builds and one diff to plot drift.
history.allSeriesHidden: All series hidden. Click a legend entry to bring one back.
history.noDriftData: No drift data for the picked mode.

driftCumulative.title: Cumulative Drift Since Baseline
driftCumulative.ariaLabel: Cumulative drift composition since the oldest published build. Reassigned, newly mapped, unmapped, and total drift series.
info: How far each build has wandered from the oldest published build that has an unfilled variant. The lines grow over time, so a flat stretch means the source data did not change against the baseline and a steep slope means it changed fast.

info.text: Prefix was mapped in both builds but now lands on a different autonomous system. Usually the largest slice. It reflects real BGP and RPKI churn.
Prefix had no autonomous system in the baseline and now resolves to one. It reflects coverage growing as upstream data adds prefixes.
Prefix that resolved in the baseline no longer resolves. It reflects upstream data dropping prefixes, usually because RPKI or IRR coverage was pulled back.
Sum of the three categories. The headline distance from the baseline build, in a single number, for the build you have selected.

info: The drift unit picker at the top of History switches every chart between IPv4 (share of routable IPv4 space whose ASN moved) and IPv6 (the same for IPv6). The two are kept apart because Bitcoin Core peer diversity treats them as independent. IPv6 is larger than IPv4 by 2 to the power of 96, so a combined denominator would round IPv4 down to noise.

Click any legend entry to hide that line and rescale the chart to what is left. Builds without an unfilled variant carry no dot, and the tooltip flags them as having no diff. The line bridges across them so the trend stays readable.

driftStep.title: Drift Between Builds
driftStep.ariaLabel: Step drift composition between each build and its last diffable predecessor. Reassigned, newly mapped, unmapped, and total drift series.
info: How much each build changed compared to the previous build that has an unfilled variant. Each release shows as one stacked bar. A tall bar is a release that moved a lot of prefixes. A flat bar is a quiet release.

info.text: Prefix was mapped in both builds but now lands on a different autonomous system between the two sides.
Prefix that was unmapped in the previous build now resolves to an autonomous system.
Prefix that resolved in the previous build no longer resolves.
Height of the full stack. In IPv4 mode this matches the headline number on the Drift vs previous overview card for the same pair.

info: The drift unit picker at the top of History switches this chart between IPv4 and IPv6 coverage. Click any legend entry to hide that category and rescale. Builds without an unfilled variant have no comparable previous build and show as an empty column. The diff is taken against the last build that actually published an unfilled variant.

entriesChart.title: Source Data Entries Over Time
entriesChart.infoAria: About the entries over time chart
entriesChart.ariaLabel: ASmap entries count over time, embedded vs source data variant. Hover the chart for exact values per build.
entriesChart.noPublishedVariants: No published variants for the loaded builds.
info: Number of prefix to AS entries each ASmap build carries. It tracks coverage growth in plain numbers, next to the per release growth bars below.

info.text: Raw upstream prefix entries the build was made from. It grows steadily as new prefixes get advertised by upstream routing data.
Entries after the fill heuristic folds neighbouring prefixes that share an AS into single rows. Always lower than unfilled. The gap between the two lines shows the heuristic compression at a glance.

info: Hover any build for the entry counts, the matching size on disk in MB, and the fill compression ratio between the two variants. Builds that did not publish a variant carry no dot, and the tooltip names them as not published, but the line connects the points around them so the trend stays readable.

mapDeltaChart.title: Source Data Entries Delta Between Builds
mapDeltaChart.ariaLabel: Source data entry count delta between each ASmap build and its last diffable predecessor. Hover each bar for details.
mapDeltaChart.empty: Need at least two builds with an unfilled variant to plot deltas.
info: Gain or loss in source data prefix entries between each build and the most recent earlier build that published an unfilled variant.

A positive bar means this build added that many real prefix to ASN entries on top of the build it is diffed against. A negative bar means entries fell out of the upstream data, usually because RPKI or IRR coverage was pulled back for some prefix.

Worked out from the unfilled (source data) variant of both sides. Builds whose immediate predecessor is filled only fall back to the last build that did publish an unfilled variant, so a single filled only release no longer blanks out the bars around it. Builds without an unfilled variant of their own still show as an empty column.
Hover any bar for the exact entry counts and the date of the build the diff was taken against.

diversityChart.title: Peer Bucket Diversity
diversityChart.ariaLabel: Distinct autonomous systems mapped per ASmap build. Each AS is one peer diversity bucket in Bitcoin Core.
diversityChart.empty: Need at least two builds to plot diversity.
info: Number of distinct autonomous systems each ASmap build maps to at least one prefix. Every AS in this number is one peer diversity bucket that Bitcoin Core can keep a connection to under the asmap based GetGroup() rule.

Coverage drift answers how much address space moved. Roster drift answers how many peer buckets the network has to work with. A diff that only reassigns prefixes between existing ASes does not change this line. A diff that adds even one prefix mapped to a brand new ASN moves this line up by one.

Worked out from the unfilled (source data) variant of each build, so the curve reflects real upstream routing data and not the fill heuristic. Filled only builds carry no dot and are bridged across by the line.

network.noData: No data for the visible series.
network.allSeriesHidden: All series hidden. Click a legend entry to bring one back.

overview.sectionLede: How the ASmap history behaves against observed Bitcoin nodes
overview.empty: No observed node snapshots in metrics.json.
overview.sourceCompare: {sources} is shown alongside as a frozen archive (its crawler no longer runs).

nodes.label: Reachable nodes
info: Distinct clearnet IP addresses the crawler reached in its most recent snapshot. Onion, I2P, and CJDNS peers are left out because they have no IP that ASmap can resolve to an autonomous system.

concentration.label: AS concentration
info: The most intuitive reading: pick two observed nodes at random, and this number is the chance they sit in the same autonomous system. At 0.02 that chance is 2 percent.

Formally it is the Herfindahl-Hirschman index (HHI), a standard measure of how concentrated something is across the players in it. Here the players are the autonomous systems the observed nodes resolve to under the ASmap build in effect.

It is computed by taking each autonomous system's share of all mapped nodes, squaring it (a 10 percent operator adds 0.01), and adding the squares together. Squaring is what makes it a concentration measure: a few large operators dominate the total while the long tail of small ones barely moves it.

The index runs from near 0, when nodes are spread evenly across very many operators, up to 1, when every node sits in a single autonomous system. A lower number therefore means healthier peer diversity.

HHI leads here instead of the single largest operator's share because that share can fall while concentration is just handed to the number-two operator. Summing over the whole distribution, HHI only drops when nodes genuinely spread across more operators. As a rule of thumb, 1 divided by the HHI is the effective number of equal-sized operators the network behaves like, so a low index like this one works out to several dozen.

buckets.label: Peer diversity buckets
info: Distinct peer diversity buckets the observed node set falls into when ASmap is loaded. Bitcoin Core's GetGroup() returns the autonomous system, or a default group for nodes that ASmap does not map.

ASmap folds many small default groups into far fewer AS buckets. That is the security gain. An adversary now has to control whole autonomous systems, not just neighbouring /16 ranges, to dominate a peer's set of buckets. Below you can see how much smaller the count is than Core's default /16 and /32 bucketing.

staleness.label: Map staleness
staleness.unit: of today's nodes shift AS per year
staleness.basis: scaled from {drift} measured at the build that is {days} days old
staleness.basisInterpolated: between {driftLower} at {daysLower} days and {driftUpper} at {daysUpper} days of map age
info: The decay curve below, distilled into a single yearly rate: the share of today's node set an embedded map would mislocate after one year of aging.

It is read off the decay curve at exactly one year of map age. Releases do not come out on a fixed schedule, so there is rarely a build exactly 365 days old; the value is interpolated between the two builds closest to either side of that mark, which keeps the reading on the measured curve. Only when the build history does not span a full year is the nearest build's drift scaled to a yearly rate instead. The line below names the readings this rests on.

This feeds straight into the question of how often Bitcoin Core should ship a fresh asmap. A low yearly rate means an embedded map ages gracefully, a high one is a reason to refresh more often. The rate here is low, so a shipped map stays close to reality for today's nodes well past its release.

charts.sectionLede: Each chart shows the available crawlers together. Click a legend entry to isolate one.

decay.title: Map decay for today's nodes
decay.ariaLabel: Share of today's observed nodes that each older build resolves to a different autonomous system than the newest build. One line per crawler.
info: We hold the most recent node set fixed and look every node up in each published build, then plot the share that lands on a different autonomous system than the newest build does. Older builds sit on the left, so a line that falls toward zero on the right means the fresher the map, the less it disagrees with reality.

The node set is held fixed across every point on purpose. That way map age is the only thing that changes. Comparing different crawl populations is what makes a plain churn line look all over the place.
Each crawler is one line. Where they overlap, the map ages the same way no matter who observed the network, which is a good check that the two data sources tell the same story.

hhi.title: AS concentration (HHI) over time
hhi.ariaLabel: Herfindahl-Hirschman index of the observed node set across every snapshot, scored against the build in effect at each one. One line per crawler. Lower means more diverse.
info: Pick two observed nodes at random, and this index is the chance they sit in the same autonomous system — at 0.02 that chance is 2 percent.

It is the same Herfindahl-Hirschman index as the concentration card above, scored against the build in effect at each snapshot instead of only the latest one. Lower is more diverse, so a line falling toward the right means the observed network keeps spreading across more operators over time.

Each crawler is its own line. They track each other closely and both drift down over the period, so the shift toward a more diverse network holds no matter who observed it, which is the cross-check the KIT only operator breakdown below cannot make on its own.

concentration.operatorsAria: Stacked bars, one per KIT snapshot, segmented into that snapshot's five largest operators by node share. The bar height is the combined top-5 share. Hover a bar for the operator names.
operatorsInfo: One bar per snapshot, split into the five operators that actually held the largest node share at that point in time, scored against the build in effect. The bar height is the combined top-5 share, so a shrinking bar means the top tier as a whole is losing grip on the network.

The top five are re-elected for every snapshot. That matters: summing a fixed set of five across the whole period would quietly understate the combined share whenever a rising operator enters the tier. Each operator keeps the same colour in every bar it appears in, so a colour vanishing or appearing along the way is exactly the tier reshuffling. Hover a bar to see who its five are.

KIT only, because this is the live crawl; the KIT and Bitnodes cross-check lives in the decay chart above. The familiar names, typically Hetzner, OVH, Google, and AWS, hold the tier throughout, and the slow decline of the bar heights while the effective operator count rises is the diversity story: the top tier is loosening rather than the lead just changing hands.

crosscheck.label: ASN attribution agreement
crosscheck.note: About {pct} of the nodes the {source} crawl tagged with their own ASN match the ASmap lookup for the same IP. Both crawlers stay in a {min} to {max} band. Open the table below for the exact counts.
crosscheck.tableToggle: Show the numbers for each snapshot
info: This is a data quality check, not a measure of network health. Of the nodes a crawler tagged with its own ASN (resolved through whois), it is the share where that ASN matches the one the ASmap build in effect assigns to the same IP address.

The few percent that do not match are expected. Attribution based on whois and attribution based on routing often differ on prefixes with more than one origin and on recent reassignments. A steady figure here means the two independent views agree, which is evidence that the data behind every chart above is sound.

Only snapshots whose crawler shipped an ASN can be scored. KIT ships one for every peer and covers the whole history. The Bitnodes archive carried whois data through late 2025, and those rows are in the table too, but its later snapshots only carry an ip and a port, so the headline figure follows KIT.

diff.needTwoUnfilled: Need at least two builds with an unfilled variant to compare.

results.samePair: Pick two different maps to see what changed.
results.unavailable: No precomputed diff for this pair. One of the two builds is missing its unfilled (source data) variant.
info: The IPv4 and IPv6 toggle next to the section title scopes everything below it, the match banner, the bucket counts, the stacked bar, and the top movers table, to one address family at a time. Bitcoin Core peer diversity treats the two families as independent, so a diff that moves 10000 IPv4 prefixes has a different operational meaning than one that moves 10000 IPv6 prefixes.

The match banner counts in peer diversity buckets, /16 buckets for IPv4 and /32 buckets for IPv6. These are the buckets Bitcoin Core's GetGroup() uses for peer diversity when no asmap is loaded. A bucket counts as changed when at least one prefix inside it gained, lost, or swapped its ASN between Map A and Map B, so the same bucket scale applies to both families.
Within the selected family, each entry level change between Map A and Map B falls into exactly one of three groups.

info.text: A prefix was mapped in both Map A and Map B but now lands on a different autonomous system. This is where most ASmap edits land.
A prefix had no autonomous system in Map A and now resolves to one in Map B.
A prefix that resolved to an autonomous system in Map A no longer resolves to one in Map B.

The footer line under the stacked bar tracks how many distinct autonomous systems each build maps, plus how many appeared (in Map B but not Map A) and disappeared (in Map A but not Map B). The roster delta does not care about address family. It counts ASes across both families, so the toggle does not change this line.

info: Worked out from the unfilled (source data) variant of each build, so each change is a real BGP, RPKI, or IRR shift and not a fill heuristic artefact.

topMovers.title: Top Movers
topMovers.empty: No top movers in the selected diff.
topMovers.noMatches: No autonomous systems match this filter.
info: Autonomous systems most affected by the selected diff, ranked by the address space they gained or lost in the active address family.

The IPv4 and IPv6 toggle next to the Diff Explorer section title scopes this table too. Each AS shows once per family, and the same AS can rank very differently in the two views. A peer that reassigns a single large IPv4 prefix barely moves the IPv6 ranking, and an AS that reshuffles dozens of tiny IPv6 blocks barely shows up under IPv4. That difference is the whole reason the two are not merged.

info.text: This AS share of the total address space the diff moved in the active currency. Hover a row for the raw IPv4 address or IPv6 /32 block count.

An up arrow means it gained from the counterpart, a down arrow means it lost to it, and a double arrow means address space moved both ways. The counterpart can be a real autonomous system or the unmapped pool. A row marked up arrow unmapped gained address space that previously had no ASN. A row marked down arrow unmapped lost address space that no longer resolves to any ASN.

info: Hover any row for the AS operator name, the raw IPv4 address or IPv6 /32 block count moved, the AS footprint on each side, and the touched ratio. Touched is the share of this AS own footprint that the diff visited. Values above 100 percent show up when one build packs the AS into a single large block while the other splits the same range into many smaller pieces.

filter.placeholder: Filter by AS number or operator

tooltip.exchangedUnmapped: exchanged addresses with the unmapped pool
tooltip.newlyMapped: gained addresses from the unmapped pool (newly mapped)
tooltip.unmapped: addresses no longer resolve to any ASN
